data.source: Online survey of American participants (primarily recruited via MTurk)
data.wave_or_subset: Three studies conducted between March 19–20, 2020, with a longitudinal subset of 193 participants who had completed a survey in October 2019
data.sample_size: 1,195 total across the three studies (e.g., Study 1 n=346; Study 2 n=537; Study 3 resampled participants)
data.notes: Samples are U.S.-based and collected online, limiting generalizability.

method.description: The authors developed and validated a 10-item Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale to measure symbolic and realistic threats. Participants completed this and other psychological questionnaires online. The scale’s two-factor structure was evaluated with principal component analysis (PCA) and confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), followed by longitudinal invariance testing. Convergent, divergent, and criterion validity were assessed through correlations and structural equation modeling (SEM) linking threat perceptions to anxiety, well-being, and adherence to public health behaviors.
steps: Recruit U.S. adult participants via MTurk and online panels for three separate survey studies.

Administer the Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale and additional measures (anxiety, life satisfaction, affect, adherence to CDC guidelines, support for public health initiatives, identity-affirming behaviors, personality and value scales).
Conduct PCA with Varimax rotation to explore factor structure.
Run CFA to compare two-factor versus one-factor models of threat perception.
Test configural, metric, and scalar invariance over time with longitudinal CFA using the SemTools package in R.
Evaluate convergent and divergent validity through correlations with need for cognitive closure, belief in a dangerous world, emotional stability, and Schwartz values.

Estimate SEMs to assess criterion validity, relating symbolic and realistic threat scores to psychological and behavioral outcomes while controlling for covariates such as pre-pandemic anxiety and political ideology.
Interpret model fit indices, standardized coefficients, and p-values to draw substantive conclusions.
method.models: Principal Component Analysis, Confirmatory Factor Analysis, Structural Equation Modeling (including longitudinal CFA)
method.outcome_variable: Adherence to public health guidelines, support for public health initiatives, anxiety, COVID-19 impact avoidance and intrusion, life satisfaction, positive/negative affect, identity-affirming behaviors
method.independent_variables: Perceived symbolic threat score, perceived realistic threat score
method.control_variables: Pre-pandemic anxiety, political ideology, demographic variables, personality/value measures where specified
method.tools_software: R (including SemTools package) and standard psychometric/statistical procedures

results.summary: Both symbolic and realistic COVID-19 threats predicted greater psychological distress, yet they influenced public health behaviors differently: realistic threat increased adherence to guidelines and support for mitigation policies, whereas symbolic threat decreased adherence and encouraged identity-affirming behaviors. The Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale demonstrated a stable two-factor structure with good psychometric properties and temporal invariance.

metadata.original_paper_title: Measuring Two Distinct Psychological Threats of COVID-19 and their Unique Impacts on Well-being and Adherence to Public Health Behaviors
